title: The Book of Various Heresies, Chapters 29–43: The Gnostic Schools
author: Filastrius of Brescia
description: The first English translation of any part of Filastrius’s catalog of heresies. These fifteen chapters cover every major Gnostic school known to the late fourth-century Church: the Simonians, Basilideans, Valentinians, Marcosians, Carpocratians, and the Barbelo-Gnostics.

title: Epic of Gilgamesh, Tablet XI: The Flood
author: Sin-leqi-unninni
description: The oldest flood narrative in world literature, predating Genesis by over a millennium. Utnapishtim tells Gilgamesh how the gods destroyed humanity with a great flood, and how he alone was granted immortality. Contains the earliest meditation on human mortality and the question of whether divinity can be shared with mortals.

title: Gospel of John, Chapter 1:1–18 (The Logos Prologue)
author: John (attributed)
description: The Prologue of John's Gospel from the earliest complete New Testament manuscript. The foundational text for Logos theology and the starting point for the theosis debate. Contains the key variant: Codex Sinaiticus reads 'only-begotten God' (monogenes theos) where later manuscripts read 'only-begotten Son.'

title: Gospel of John 10:22–39: “I Said, You Are Gods”
author: John (attributed)
description: The most direct confrontation in the Gospels over Christ's divinity. Jesus declares 'I and the Father are one,' is accused of blasphemy, and responds by quoting Psalm 82: 'I said, you are gods.' The single most contested passage in the theosis debate.

title: 2 Peter 1:3–4: “Partakers of the Divine Nature”
author: Peter (attributed)
description: The most explicit theosis statement in the New Testament: 'that you may become partakers of the divine nature.' The scriptural foundation for the entire Eastern Orthodox doctrine of divinization.

title: On the Incarnation, Chapter 54: “He Became Man That We Might Become God”
author: Athanasius of Alexandria
description: The most famous sentence in the history of the theosis doctrine. Athanasius argues that the entire purpose of the Incarnation was not merely to save humanity from sin but to transform human nature: to make the mortal immortal, the human divine.

title: Gospel of John 17:20-26: The High Priestly Prayer
author: John (attributed)
description: Jesus's prayer for all future believers: "that they all may be one, just as you, Father, are in me and I in you, that they also may be in us." The most explicit statement of mutual indwelling in the New Testament, extending the divine-human unity to all believers.

title: Gospel of Luke 1:1-4: The Historian's Prologue
author: Luke (attributed)
description: Luke's prologue is the earliest statement of historical methodology in Christian literature. He tells Theophilus he has investigated everything carefully from eyewitnesses — a statement of source verification that mirrors the mission of this library.

title: Gospel of Mark 1:1-11: The Beginning and the Baptism
author: Mark (attributed)
description: The opening of the earliest Gospel. No birth narrative — straight to John the Baptist and the baptism, where the heavens are torn open, the Spirit descends, and the voice declares: "You are my beloved Son." The moment the divine enters the human.

title: Gospel of Matthew 5:43-48: "Be Perfect As Your Father Is Perfect"
author: Matthew (attributed)
description: The climax of the Sermon on the Mount's ethical teaching. "Be perfect (teleioi) as your heavenly Father is perfect." The word teleios means complete, whole — reaching the telos. For the theosis tradition, this is a command to become like God.

title: Refutation of All Heresies, Book 6, Chapters 24–37
author: Hippolytus of Rome
description: Hippolytus describes Valentinian Gnostic theology in detail: the divine Pleroma, the emanation of aeons, the fall of Sophia, the creation of the material world by the Demiurge, and the divine spark trapped within humanity.

title: Against Heresies, Book III, Chapter 19: "He Became What We Are"
author: Irenaeus of Lyon
description: The earliest systematic articulation of the theosis doctrine. Irenaeus argues that the Word became what we are so that we might become what he is.

title: Against the Valentinians
author: Tertullian
description: Tertullian’s sharp, satirical attack on Valentinian Gnosticism. Written entirely in Latin by one of the most vivid prose stylists in Christian history.

title: Against Arius, Book 1
author: Marius Victorinus
description: A Neoplatonist philosopher who converted to Christianity in old age, Marius Victorinus brought the full apparatus of Greek philosophical thought to bear on the question of Christ’s divinity.

title: Psalm 82: "I Said, You Are Gods"
author: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)
description: The psalm Jesus quotes at John 10:34. God stands in the divine assembly, judges the gods, and declares: "I said, you are gods." The most important Old Testament text for the theosis debate.

title: Genesis 1:26-28: "Let Us Make Man in Our Image"
author: Moses (attributed)
description: The creation of humanity in the image (eikon) and likeness (homoiosis) of God. The distinction between image and likeness became central to the theosis tradition: image is given at creation, likeness is achieved through divinization.

title: The Nicene Creed (Niceno-Constantinopolitan, 381 AD)
author: Council of Nicaea / Constantinople
description: The formal resolution of the first great theological crisis. Defines Christ as homoousios (of one substance) with the Father. For the theosis tradition, the Creed is both foundation and constraint.
